answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or repetitive activity involving your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started while I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_a6: I iced it the first day
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your affected arm?
answers.intake_a7: no just pain with lifting objects
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or arthritis?
answers.intake_summary: Patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that started while playing tennis, without swelling, numbness, or systemic symptoms. No prior elbow issues or arthritis. Pain occurs with lifting but no weakness or grip difficulty. Initial icing was tried.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and is aggravated by lifting objects, though she denies weakness or difficulty gripping. There is no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. She has no prior history of elbow problems or arthritis. Initial self-treatment included icing on the first day. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain related to repetitive wrist extension and gripping activities. The absence of swelling, systemic symptoms, or neurological signs supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen, for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen for pain control if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs in patients with allergy or intolerance. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is development of swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, weakness, or systemic symptoms such as fever. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical clinical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if diagnosis is uncertain.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Check if you have tennis elbow The main symptom of tennis elbow is pain on the outside of your elbow. The pain may range from mild discomfort when you move your arm to constant pain that can affect your sleep. It may be worse when: - lifting or bending your arm - gripping objects - moving your wrist Other symptoms of tennis elbow include: - tenderness or swe…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.title: Sports Injuries: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: ou, but sometimes you can injure yourself when you play sports or exercise. Accidents, poor training practices, or improper gear can cause them. Some people get hurt because they are not in shape. Not warming up or stretching enough can also lead to injuries. The most common sports injuries are: Sprains and strains Knee injuries Swollen muscles Achilles tend…
